narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad work and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut because our predictive model sees a 64% chance you might not pass the bar exam down the line. Picture this as the model piecing together your story like a cautious advisor looking at patterns from thousands of students who've walked this path before: the biggest red flag it spotted was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the bottom of your class academically during that critical later stage, which strongly pulled the prediction toward failure more than anything else. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 33.0, which, while solid for some, signaled to the model a tougher climb ahead in mastering the intense bar material. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 echoed that early struggle, reinforcing a pattern of grades that didn't climb as hoped. Coming from a middle family income bracket added another layer of concern, as the model associates that with fewer resources for the grueling prep many successful bar-takers rely on. Your Asian background, male gender, undergraduate GPA of 3.0, and full-time enrollment plans all nudged the scales further toward risk in their own ways, painting a picture of someone facing steeper odds in a field where these traits have historically correlated with bar challenges. It's not that you're incapable—far from it—but the model weighs this unique combination as a cautionary tale, urging schools to prioritize profiles with smoother trajectories. The silver lining? This is a wake-up call; targeting law full-force like you are (that target_law drive is clear) means you can still rewrite this story with targeted improvements in study habits or retakes. You've got the determination—use this to fuel your next steps. Hang in there; many have turned similar predictions around.
